intro: Calculate how much to feed your dog each day based on weight, life stage, and food calorie density. Uses the veterinary RER (Resting Energy Requirement) formula recommended by the Association of American Feed Control Officials (AAFCO) for accurate daily portion guidance.
howItWorks: Resting Energy Requirement (RER) = 70 × weight(kg)^0.75. The RER is then multiplied by a life stage factor: puppies 2.0×, adult neutered dogs 1.6×, active dogs 2.0×, seniors 1.4×, pregnant/nursing 3.0×. Daily grams = Daily Calories ÷ Food Calories per 100g × 100. One dry kibble cup is approximately 113g, though this varies by brand.
interpretationGuide: These are guidelines, not exact prescriptions. Adjust by 10% up or down based on your dog's body condition score. You should be able to feel (but not see) ribs easily. If your dog is gaining weight, reduce by 10%. If losing weight unintentionally, increase by 10% and consult a vet. Always check the feeding guide on your specific food bag — different kibbles have very different calorie densities.

faqs.question: How often should I feed my dog?
faqs.answer: Adult dogs typically do well on 2 meals per day (morning and evening). Puppies under 6 months should eat 3–4 times daily to maintain blood sugar. Large breed puppies can be reduced to 2 meals at 6 months. Some dogs do fine on one meal, but two meals reduces bloat risk in large breeds.

faqs.question: What does RER mean?
faqs.answer: RER stands for Resting Energy Requirement — the calories a dog needs at complete rest in a thermoneutral environment. It represents baseline metabolism. Actual daily needs are higher based on life stage and activity, which is why we multiply RER by a life stage factor.

faqs.question: How do I find the calories per 100g in my dog's food?
faqs.answer: Check the guaranteed analysis or feeding guide on the bag. Look for 'kcal/kg' or 'kcal per cup'. Divide kcal/kg by 10 to get kcal per 100g. Typical dry kibble ranges from 300–450 kcal/100g; wet food is usually 60–120 kcal/100g.

faqs.question: How much should a puppy eat?
faqs.answer: Puppies need about 2× the calories per kg of an adult dog of the same breed. Feed puppy-specific food until growth is complete — typically 12 months for small breeds, 18–24 months for large and giant breeds. Split the daily ration into 3–4 meals.

faqs.question: How do I know if my dog is at a healthy weight?
faqs.answer: Use the Body Condition Score (BCS): on a 1–9 scale, ideal is 4–5. At a healthy weight: ribs easily felt under light pressure but not visible; visible waist when viewed from above; abdomen tucking up when viewed from the side. Consult your vet if uncertain.

faqs.question: Should I feed wet or dry food?
faqs.answer: Both can be nutritionally complete if they meet AAFCO standards. Dry kibble is convenient and good for dental health. Wet food has higher moisture (good for dogs that don't drink enough) and is often more palatable. Many owners mix both. If mixing, adjust portions so total calories meet the daily target.

faqs.question: How much water should my dog drink?
faqs.answer: A healthy dog needs approximately 50–60ml of water per kg of body weight per day. A 20kg dog needs roughly 1–1.2 litres. Dry kibble diets require more water intake than wet food diets. Puppies, pregnant, and nursing dogs need more.

faqs.question: Do treats count toward daily calories?
faqs.answer: Yes — treats should not exceed 10% of daily caloric intake. Many owners forget treats when measuring food and wonder why their dog is gaining weight. Calculate treat calories and reduce the main meal portion accordingly.

faqs.question: Should senior dogs eat less?
faqs.answer: Senior dogs typically need 20–30% fewer calories because they are less active. However, they may need higher protein to maintain muscle mass. Use a senior-formulated food and the 'senior' setting in this calculator. Monitor weight monthly and adjust.

faqs.question: How do I transition my dog to new food?
faqs.answer: Transition over 7–10 days to avoid digestive upset: Days 1–3: 25% new food, 75% old. Days 4–6: 50/50. Days 7–9: 75% new, 25% old. Day 10: 100% new food. Transition slower for sensitive dogs.

faqs.question: Can I feed my dog a raw diet using this calculator?
faqs.answer: This calculator works for any food type if you know the calorie density. Raw diets vary widely — typically 150–250 kcal/100g for commercial raw food. Weigh the food rather than using cup measurements for raw diets. Consult a veterinary nutritionist for raw diet formulation.

faqs.question: What if my dog won't finish their meals?
faqs.answer: If your dog regularly leaves food, reduce the portion by 10–15%. If they inhale food and always seem hungry, check their BCS — they may genuinely need more, or they may be at a healthy weight and just greedy. Slow feeders and puzzle bowls can help dogs that eat too fast.
